summary:
Çatalhöyük was one of the largest and best-preserved Neolithic settlements in the world, located on the Konya Plain in south-central Anatolia (modern Turkey). Occupied from approximately 7500 to 5700 BCE, the site reached a peak population estimated between 5,000 and 10,000 inhabitants at its height, making it among the most densely occupied communities of the prehistoric world. Its discovery and excavation by James Mellaart in the 1950s and 1960s transformed scholarly understanding of early urban life, and subsequent work led by Ian Hodder from the 1990s onward added exceptional detail to what daily existence meant in this proto-urban environment.

The settlement's most distinctive architectural feature was its unique house-building practice: dwellings were constructed immediately adjacent to one another with no streets between them. Inhabitants entered their homes through holes in the roof, descending via wooden ladders. When a house deteriorated, it was filled with debris and a new one built directly on top, creating the characteristic mound that archaeologists call a höyük or tepe. This layered accumulation, reaching up to eighteen meters in depth across dozens of occupation levels, provides an extraordinarily detailed stratigraphic record of community life over roughly 1,800 years.

The interior walls of many houses at Çatalhöyük were decorated with elaborate [[event:28|wall paintings and plaster reliefs]], featuring geometric patterns, hunting scenes, and depictions of wild animals — particularly the aurochs and leopard. [[event:1466|Bull skulls and horns]] were frequently plastered into walls and benches, reflecting a rich symbolic world centered on wild animals. Beneath the floors, the settlement's inhabitants buried their dead, sometimes including elaborate grave goods, suggesting complex beliefs about ancestry and the afterlife consistent with [[event:1467|ancestor worship traditions]] of the period.

Çatalhöyük's economy was based on a mixed strategy of cultivated cereals and legumes supplemented by hunting of wild cattle, red deer, and other game. Evidence of [[event:14|wheat and barley cultivation]] is abundant throughout the site, alongside animal bones reflecting continued reliance on hunting even as domestication progressed. The settlement also engaged in long-distance exchange, obtaining obsidian from volcanic sources in Cappadocia and shells from the Mediterranean coast, demonstrating that even this early community participated in networks of exchange spanning hundreds of kilometers.
